title: Microglial Morphological Transitions in the Aging Mouse Cortex
impact: First systematic atlas of age-dependent microglial shape change
problem: Microglia were treated as a homogenous population; age-related morphological diversity was completely uncharacterised.
method: Confocal morphometry across 6 age groups (2–28 months) in C57BL/6J mice to build a quantitative shape atlas.
results: Identified 4 distinct morphological states; ramified to ameboid transition accelerated after 18 months with strong regional specificity.
impact_long: Foundational reference cited in over 120 papers; established the aging cortex as a shifting microglial habitat.

title: p38-MAPK Drives Microglial Senescence: A Neuroinflammatory Tipping Point
impact: Pinpointed the molecular switch for microglial burnout in aging brains
problem: No causal molecular pathway linked normal aging to the chronic low-grade inflammation seen in aged brains.
method: Conditional p38-MAPK knockout in microglia combined with aged mouse models and LPS challenge assays.
results: p38 deletion halved IL-6 and TNF-α secretion in 24-month mice; rescued phagocytic capacity by 40%.
impact_long: Identified the first druggable microglial target for neuroinflammation; spawned 3 independent pharma discovery programs.

title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Landscape of Disease-Associated Microglia in Early Alzheimer's
impact: Mapped the DAM state at single-cell resolution; redrew the glial cell atlas
problem: Bulk RNA-seq masked rare microglial sub-populations critical to disease onset; DAM heterogeneity remained speculative.
method: 10x Genomics scRNA-seq of 48,000 microglia from 5xFAD mice at pre-plaque, early-plaque and late-plaque stages.
results: Resolved 9 distinct microglial clusters; found 2 novel TREM2-low states preceding plaque accumulation.
impact_long: Dataset downloaded over 3,400 times; DAM cluster annotations used directly in human GWAS studies.

title: Synaptic Pruning Dysfunction in Aged Microglia: Complement-Driven Loss
impact: Explained why aged brains lose synapses faster — and how to slow it
problem: Synapse loss in aging was well-documented but its glial mechanistic driver remained completely unknown.
method: CR3 and C1q conditional knockdown in aged microglia combined with two-photon in vivo spine imaging over 3 weeks.
results: Blocking the C1q–CR3 axis reduced synapse elimination by 55% in 20-month mice; preserved spatial memory on water maze.
impact_long: Opened the complement pathway as a therapeutic avenue; two active clinical trials cite this work directly.

title: Microglial Heterogeneity Across Brain Regions Shapes Regional Vulnerability to Neurodegeneration
impact: Explained why some brain regions degenerate first — it is the microglia
problem: Regional susceptibility in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's lacked any glial explanation; cortex versus substantia nigra divergence was a mystery.
method: Spatial transcriptomics (Visium) combined with ATAC-seq across 8 brain regions in human post-mortem and matched mouse tissue.
results: Found region-specific chromatin accessibility programs; limbic microglia carry vulnerability signatures even at baseline.
impact_long: Changed how neuropathologists interpret regional degeneration patterns; curriculum-referenced in 4 universities worldwide.

title: A Senolytics Strategy for Clearing Senescent Microglia Restores Cognition in Aged Mice
impact: First proof-of-concept that clearing aged microglia reverses cognitive decline
problem: Therapeutic strategies targeted amyloid and tau but ignored the immune landscape entirely; senescent microglia as targets were completely untested.
method: ABT-263 navitoclax treatment in 22-month mice with BV2 cell line validation; Morris Water Maze and NOR cognition assays.
results: 35% reduction in p21 and p16 positive microglia; significant recovery in both spatial memory and executive function tests.
impact_long: Covered in Nature News and Science Daily; translated interest from 6 biotech firms for active drug repurposing programs.
